course_description:
Hypersonic gas dynamics including: hypersonic wind tunnels, flow deflection techniques, viscous flows, non-equilibrium flows, rarefied gas dynamics, and satellite drag.

This is a capstone aerospace engineering course which focuses on the important topic of high-speed flight at hypersonic speeds - travel at faster than 5 times the speed of sound. Hypersonic flight is important for many scenarios such as launching payloads into orbit, returning to Earth from space, entry into the atmospheres of other planets, or sustained high-speed flight in the Earth's atmosphere.

This course covers important hypersonic concepts such as high-temperature gas dynamics, flow deflection techniques, viscous flows, hypersonic wind tunnels, and the rarefied gas dynamics experienced high up in the atmosphere or in space.

course_contacts.notes: Please email Dr James to request a consultation.

UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: The aim of this course is to equip you with the tools to analyse, evaluate, and design solutions for hypersonic vehicles, which are challenged by their operation at Mach numbers much greater than one.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain the unique design issues that need to be considered when designing hypersonic vehicles.
Determine the most appropriate type of hypersonic test facility for a given hypersonic flow scenario and evaluate the test conditions generated in that facility.
Explain how gases behave in the high temperature environment experienced during hypersonic flow and how this influences hypersonic vehicle design.
Evaluate the thermodynamic properties of high temperature gases.
Evaluate the heating and forces experienced by hypersonic vehicles.
Evaluate the effect of viscous boundary layer flow on hypersonic vehicles.
Estimate the degree of rarefaction of gas flows and explain why it is important to be able to characterise these flows in this manner.
Evaluate the forces and heating experienced by vehicles and spacecraft in low density, rarefied flows.

assessment_summary.title: Shock Tunnel Laboratory Experiment
assessment_summary.due_date: 13/04/2026 - 24/04/2026; Lab reports are due by 14:00 four calendar weeks after the date that the experiment was performed.

assessment_details.title: In-class Problems
assessment_details.task_description: Applied Class problems will be issued on Blackboard to students just before the scheduled Applied Class. The staff running the Applied Class will host the class in person at the scheduled time. The teaching staff will answer questions and explain the task to be completed individually during the Applied Class. Solutions to some or all of the Applied Class problems will be presented and discussed during the course of the Applied Class. The assessment for each Applied Class question is full marks (worth 1%), part marks (worth 0.5%), or zero marks (worth 0%). The total mark is then the mean average of your mark for each of the questions for that week. Full marks requires that you make a reasonable attempt at the question by selecting appropriate theory and methodology from the lecture material and applying it to the question. Even if your final answer may be incorrect and/or you have made some mistakes, you will still have attempted most of the question in some form. Part marks is awarded if an appropriate but limited attempt is performed, such as completing only a small amount of the question. Zero marks would constitute either not doing a question at all or very incorrectly attempting the question, such as applying an inappropriate analysis to the question. Work must be submitted individually, however, students are actively encouraged to collaborate with group members in completing this learning activity. Submission is online via Blackboard at 2:00 PM the next business day after the Applied Class. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission is online via Blackboard at 14:00 the day after the applied class.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
The maximum mark which can be obtained for this item of assessment is 10% (i.e. if you submit 9 tutorials during the course of the semester and receive full marks for each, your final mark for this item of assessment will be 9%; whereas if you submit 12 tutorials during the course of the semester and receive full marks for each, your final mark for this item of assessment will be 10%). This is designed to accommodate unforeseen circumstances such as illness which may mean that students are not able to submit every tutorial during the semester. If extenuating circumstances means that a student has had to miss many weeks of tutorials, marks may be able to be scaled based on the results for the amount of tutorials that the student was able to submit.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Solutions to the Applied Class problems will be presented and discussed during the course of the Applied Class.

assessment_details.title: Shock Tunnel Laboratory Experiment
assessment_details.due_date: 13/04/2026 - 24/04/2026 Lab reports are due by 14:00 four calendar weeks after the date that the experiment was performed.
assessment_details.task_description: Experiments performed in the UQ 'Drummond' reflected shock tunnel. A reflected shock tunnel is a common type of hypersonic test facility used to further the understanding of hypersonic flow. A teaching assistant will perform experiments on the facility in front of the students and students will analyse provided data using techniques taught in class. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Please refer to Blackboard for the marking criteria. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission is online via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
As the Practical component is timed-assessment, Student Access Plans (SAPs) and discretionary requests are not suitable supporting documentation. Students must provide evidence that demonstrates their inability to attend the assessment at the scheduled time.
Students unable to attend their scheduled practical must be able to attend another practical.
Students submitting an extension must indicate whether the extension request is for the practical component or the report component.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
A 100% late penalty applies for non-attendance at practicals without prior approval as detailed in extension or deferral availability.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 12 (23 Feb - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Applied Classes This time is set aside for solving example problems and includes time to ask questions about the course content, the practical, and the assignment. Applied Class problems will be issued on Blackboard to students just before the scheduled Applied Class time. The teaching staff will answer questions and explain the task to be completed individually during the Applied Class. Solutions to some or all of the Applied Class problems will be presented and discussed during the course of the Applied Class. Work must be submitted individually, however, students are actively encouraged to collaborate with group members in completing this learning activity. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
Introduction Introduction to hypersonics and normal shock waves Learning outcomes: L01, L05
Hypersonic Test Facilities Introduction to hypersonic ground testing and the basic shock tube. Learning outcomes: L02
Hypersonic Test Facilities Reflected shock tunnels, scaling for hypersonic experiments, and the expansion tube. Learning outcomes: L02

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 5 (16 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Shock Tunnel Laboratory Experiment In this practical a teaching assistant will perform experiments in a small reflected shock tunnel, a common hypersonic test facility. After the practical students will compare and evaluate the results in the context of the theory presented in class. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
High Temperature Gas Dynamics Introduction to high temperature gases and statistical thermodynamics. Learning outcomes: L03, L04
High Temperature Gas Dynamics Thermochemical equilibrium mixtures. Learning outcomes: L03, L04
High Temperature Gas Dynamics Chemical and thermal non-equilbrium. Learning outcomes: L03, L04
Surface Inclination Techniques Shock-expansion theory and Newtonian theory.
Viscous Flow Flat plate laminar viscous flow. Learning outcomes: L05, L06
Viscous Flow Stagnation point heating and experimental techniques. Learning outcomes: L05, L06
Rarefied Gas Dynamics Introduction to rarified gas dynamics and kinetic theory. Learning outcomes: L07, L08
Rarefied Gas Dynamics Rarified gas drag and heat transfer (part 1). Learning outcomes: L07, L08
Rarefied Gas Dynamics Rarified gas drag and heat transfer (part 2). Learning outcomes: L07, L08
Revision Revision of course content. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
